romania.title: Ministerul Educației a pus în consultare standardele naționale de evaluare pentru clasele I-VIII. Ce se schimbă
romania.summary: Ministerul Educației și Cercetării a lansat în consultare publică standardele naționale de evaluare pentru învățământul primar și gimnazial. Acestea descriu ce trebuie să știe și să poată face un elev la fiecare competență din programa școlară și stabilesc criterii comune după care profesorii vor evalua și vor justifica notele. Ministerul Educației și Cercetării explică faptul …

romania.title: Ce se poate schimba în evaluarea și notarea elevilor. Standardele propuse de Ministerul Educației vor sta la baza viitoarelor examene
romania.summary: Ministerul Educației a lansat în consultare publică proiectul de ordin privind aprobarea standardelor naționale de evaluare pentru învățământul primar și gimnazial, un document care introduce, pentru prima dată, criterii naționale explicite după care poate fi evaluată fiecare competență prevăzută în programele școlare.

romania.title: 😂 Piaţa maşinilor second, în scădere. VW concediază 50.000 de bătrânici care conduceau doar duminica
romania.summary: Pe fondul unei cereri din ce în ce mai reduse de maşini second-hand pe pieţe precum România, Albania sau Burkina Faso, conducerea Volkswagen e nevoită să ia măsuri dure! Directorul general al companiei, Pimmel Schwanzhoff, a anunţat recent că va disponibiliza aproape 50.000 de bătrânici care scoteau maşinile din garaj duminica pentru a merge doar […] The post Piaţa maşinilor second, în scădere. VW concediază 50.000 de bătrânici care conduceau doar duminica appeared first on Times New Roman .

romania.title: Eroul Spaniei la Cupa Davis din 2019 a anunțat că și-a încheiat cariera
romania.summary: Tenismanul spaniol Roberto Bautista Agut a disputat în acest weekend ultimul său turneu profesionist, punând astfel capăt unei cariere îndelungate, de peste două decenii, un parcurs în care a devenit eroul victoriei Spaniei în finala Cupei Davis din 2019, printre alte realizări importante, relatează agenția EFE, citată de Agerpres. Istoricul turneu Copa del Rey de …

romania.title: Mini-reactoarele NuScale de la Doicești: Directorul general Cosmin Ghiță de la Nuclearelectrica  recunoaște că statul a plătit mult mai mult decât partenerul privat
romania.summary: Acționarii Nuclearelectrica sunt chemați la ședința AGA din 15 iulie 2026 pentru a aproba căutarea unor noi tehnologii de mini-reactoare.   ​Proiectul reactoarelor modulare mici (SMR) de la Doicești este blocat.  Asta după ce termenul-limită din iunie 2026 pentru îndeplinirea condițiilor Deciziei Finale de Investiție (FID) a expirat fără semnarea unui acord-cadru cu partenerul american NuScale.  …

romania.title: Romanul „Oameni mari”, de Maria Orban, publicat în limba franceză
romania.summary: București, 13 iulie 2026. Imprintul n’autor al editurii Nemira anunță publicarea în limba franceză a romanului „Oameni mari”, de Maria Orban (2020; 2022, ed. a II-a). Traducerea este semnată de… Articolul Romanul „Oameni mari”, de Maria Orban, publicat în limba franceză apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

romania.title: „VALENTIN M-A TREZIT! – CORPUS”. O expoziție de Noni-Emil Iordache
romania.summary: Pe 16 iulie 2026, de la ora 19:00, la Hotel Caro din București, are loc vernisajul expoziției personale de pictură „Valentin m-a trezit! – CORPUS”, semnată de Noni-Emil Iordache. Uneori,… Articolul „VALENTIN M-A TREZIT! – CORPUS”. O expoziție de Noni-Emil Iordache apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

romania.title: Sit+Read POP—UP Fair aduce la Timișoara două zile dedicate creației independente și artiștilor locali
romania.summary: În perioada 1–2 august 2026, între orele 12:00 și 19:00, Indecis Artist-Run (Bastion 3, str. Hector nr. 1) găzduiește Sit+Read POP—UP Fair, un eveniment care reunește artiști, designeri și creatori… Articolul Sit+Read POP—UP Fair aduce la Timișoara două zile dedicate creației independente și artiștilor locali apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

romania.title: Orchestra Operei Naționale București, decorată cu Medalia Camerei Deputaților a Republicii Italiene. Concert de gală la Ravello Festival, dirijat de Daniel Jinga în încheierea semestrului românesc al Anului Cultural România – Italia 2026.

Concert de gală la Ravello Festival, dirijat de Daniel Jinga în încheierea semestrului românesc al Anului Cultural România – Italia 2026. apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

romania.title: Programul organizat de WIPO și Rubik Hub, WIPO IP Management Clinic, a ajuns la final
romania.summary: Organizația Mondială a Proprietății Intelectuale (WIPO), împreună cu Rubik Hub, Digital Innovation Zone (DIZ), IMAGO-MOL Cluster și Innovate EU, a lansat în martie 2026 programul WIPO x Rubik Hub IP… Articolul Programul organizat de WIPO și Rubik Hub, WIPO IP Management Clinic, a ajuns la final apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

romania.title: 😂 Elevii care au picat BAC-ul cer să poată intra la doctorat și fără BAC
romania.summary: Protest la Minsterul Educației. Mii de elevi de clasa a XII-a, care au picat BAC-ul, solicită o lege care să le dea dreptul să se înscrie la doctorat fără diploma de Bacalaurea. Ei spun că vor să facă cercetare și statul nu le permite, pentru că are legi învechite, care nu stimulează performanța academică. Mulți […] The post Elevii care au picat BAC-ul cer să poată intra la doctorat și fără BAC appeared first on Times New Roman .

romania.title: 😂 Pe Via Transilvanica au fost montate televizoare imense, să poți vedea meciuri în timp ce mergi pe jos
romania.summary: Pentru ca turiștii de pe Via Transilvanica să nu rateze meciurile de la diversele campionate de fotbal din lume, pe marginea traseului de drumeție au fost montate televizoare uriașe, alimentate cu panouri solare și baterii. Din păcate turiștii nu vor putea schimba canalele, pentru că exista riscul ca familiile cu copii să mute pe Tra La […] The post Pe Via Transilvanica au fost montate televizoare imense, să poți vedea meciuri în timp ce mergi pe jos appeared first on Times New Roman .

romania.title: Inocenți, locul în care curajul crește pas cu pas
romania.summary: Descoperă povestea Alinei și cum sprijinul oferit de Fundația Inocenți a ajutat-o să își dezvolte încrederea în sine, abilitățile școlare și curajul de a reuși. The post Inocenți, locul în care curajul crește pas cu pas appeared first on Fundația Inocenți .

romania.title: Vestul nu se lasă și vrea să creeze un nou unicorn românesc. Care sunt cele 9 startup-uri pe drumul ambițios pavat de Vest Ventures
romania.summary: Regiunea Vest a spus cu nenumărate ocazii că vrea să dea țării următorul unicorn românesc. Până în 2030. Mișcările din ultimii doi ani și fondurile recent lansate lucrează la misiunea asta la toate...

romania.summary: București, 1 iulie. Rezultatele Evaluării Naționale 2026 reconfirmă decalajele profunde dintre copiii din mediul rural și cei din mediul urban... Articolul Evaluarea Națională 2026: Tranziția către liceu, punctul critic al abandonului școlar. World Vision România propune un plan de intervenție apare prima dată în World Vision România .

romania.title: Sondaj World Vision România de Ziua Copilului: aproape un sfert dintre copii și adolescenți consultați spun că adulții îi ascultă rar sau aproape niciodată. Anxietatea și presiunea școlii, principalele probleme ale generației lor.

Articolul Sondaj World Vision România de Ziua Copilului: aproape un sfert dintre copii și adolescenți consultați spun că adulții îi ascultă rar sau aproape niciodată. Anxietatea și presiunea școlii, principalele probleme ale generației lor. apare prima dată în World Vision România .

medical.title: Novel technology could aid targeted lung cancer treatment
medical.summary: Scientists have developed a new method for accurately predicting gene changes that cause lung cancer, without the need for slower, more expensive lab techniques. The technology was able to identify specific genetic changes with high accuracy, offering a potentially faster, more efficient and cheaper testing option than traditional methods.

medical.title: Epigenetic therapy shows promise against treatment-resistant AML
medical.summary: Researchers at The University of Texas MD Anderson Cancer Center have discovered that an investigational epigenetic therapy remained effective in treatment-resistant acute myeloid leukemia (AML) by activating the Hippo pathway, a tumor-suppressing pathway linked to cancer growth and drug resistance.

medical.title: Young blood stem cells rejuvenate aging immune systems in old mice
medical.summary: By freezing your own healthy blood stem cells in your 20s, thawing them and undergoing a stem cell transplant in your 40s or 50s, it might be possible to rejuvenate your blood-forming and immune systems. Science fiction? At least it works when old mice receive healthy blood stem cells from young mice.

medical.title: Advanced climate models used to estimate temperature-related suicide patterns by 2050
medical.summary: A large international team, including researchers from the University of Tokyo, wanted to know whether and how climate change might increase the number of temperature-related suicides around the world. Previous studies have shown that hotter weather is often linked to a higher risk of suicide, but the present study, published in Nature Mental Health, combined data from an unprecedented 751 locations in 26 countries and used advanced climate models to estimate how the number of temperature-related suicides might change by the 2050s under different climate change scenarios.

medical.title: Social media likes may have a bigger influence on people with depression
medical.summary: One of the first things many people do after posting on social media is check how many likes they have and who has liked their content. This habit can be an instant mood booster when a post is popular.

medical.title: How the brain's chemical messengers control consciousness and sleep
medical.summary: Scientists at Newcastle University's Neural Circuits Laboratory, in collaboration with researchers at the Blue Brain Project (EPFL, Switzerland) and leading institutions in Spain, have published a study that advances understanding of the brain's chemical messengers—acetylcholine, dopamine and serotonin—known as neuromodulators.

medical.title: Knowledge about genes isn't enough: How to inform people about genetic risk of obesity
medical.summary: Does informing people about their genetic risk of obesity help them change their habits and lose excess weight? A study published in the journal Obesity Reviews by scientists, including researchers from SWPS University, shows that knowledge about one's genes alone is not enough. Effective behavioral change requires long-term, personalized support.

medical.title: Simple digital check-ins improve outcomes for cancer patients, study finds
medical.summary: New results from an Alliance Foundation Trials (AFT) study reveal that a simple weekly electronic symptom check-in with their care team significantly improves the quality of life for individuals undergoing treatment for advanced cancer. The study found that the biggest improvements were seen among patient groups that have historically faced the greatest barriers to care, including Black patients and individuals with less formal education.

medical.title: Researchers develop a safer, more reliable material for growing small-scale models of the human gut
medical.summary: Scientists can use patient-derived tissue samples to grow miniature versions of human organs, allowing them to test new medications and disease treatments for personalized care.

medical.title: Watching nature videos can help relieve stress, study finds
medical.summary: When stressed, it's important to find healthy ways to cope. Getting outdoors is a proven way to do so. But not everyone has easy access to nature. That's where nature videos might help, according to a North Carolina State University professor.

medical.title: Enhancing safety of advanced radiotherapy cancer treatments for children
medical.summary: Researchers from the National Physical Laboratory (NPL), Heidelberg University Hospital and the German Cancer Research Center (DKFZ) have published new findings that improve understanding of secondary neutron exposure during pediatric ion beam therapy, an important area of study for enhancing the safety of advanced cancer treatments for children.

medical.title: Cellular recycling protein plays critical protective role in the gut
medical.summary: Australian researchers have discovered that even a modest reduction in the protein BECLIN1 leads to a significant increase in gut inflammation, which can lead to further disease and complications over time.

medical.title: The importance of human review in AI-driven physicians' documentation
medical.summary: Artificial intelligence in clinical settings is becoming more common. AI speech-to-text had a moment in the spotlight on the medical drama series "The Pitt" earlier this year. "Studies show that you can spend 80% less time charting," says character Baran Al-Hashimi, a doctor showing her peers a new AI speech-to-text tool. But a skeptical colleague raised concerns after the tool misheard the name of a patient's medication.

medical.title: Insurance-supported integrative oncology program improves patients' symptoms
medical.summary: A new study from University Hospitals Connor Whole Health reports that an insurance-supported integrative oncology program resulted in clinically meaningful improvements in cancer-related symptoms like pain, stress, anxiety, depression, nausea and fatigue. The findings highlight a scalable model with potential for delivering future integrative therapies within routine oncology care. The findings from this study were recently published in JCO Oncology Practice.

medical.title: Why are healthy young non-smokers developing lung cancer?
medical.summary: An unexpected study found that young non-smokers with healthier diets had higher rates of lung cancer, raising questions about whether pesticide exposure from conventionally grown produce could play a role. Researchers stress that the findings are preliminary and require further studies before any conclusions can be drawn.

medical.title: Where you live could shape your dementia risk, massive study finds
medical.summary: Researchers analyzing data from more than 214,000 people found that dementia risk factors differ widely across countries, challenging the idea of a one-size-fits-all prevention strategy. At the same time, they uncovered surprisingly consistent patterns that could help shape smarter, more targeted public health efforts.

science.title: Random by design: Flickering genes may spend energy to achieve precision
science.summary: Inside the cell nucleus, genes must be turned on and off with precision to regulate biological processes. The first models of gene regulation were developed in the 1960s, yet modern science continues to uncover new layers of control. A new study involving researchers from the Institute of Science and Technology Austria (ISTA), the Institut Pasteur and Princeton University, published in PNAS, suggests that genes obey an optimal switching principle—random at any given moment, yet precise on average.

science.title: Scientists' credibility depends on audience and perceived motives
science.summary: A new study from Virginia Tech found that scientists' credibility often hinges on their perceived motivations. "Our study finds that perceptions of scientists' motivation to serve the public good are the strongest predictor of whether Americans trust them, accept their information as credible, and support science-based policies," said Dara Wald, associate professor in environmental policy and planning.

science.title: As super El Niño draws global attention, the Indian Ocean may hold the key to Mediterranean climate extremes
science.summary: As scientists around the world closely monitor the possible development of a powerful "Super El Niño," a new study suggests that another tropical ocean deserves equal attention. Researchers have found that temperature changes in the Indian Ocean can significantly influence winter weather thousands of kilometers away in the Eastern Mediterranean, offering new opportunities to predict damaging dry spells months before they occur.

science.title: Medieval plague survivors left us graffiti, court records and a lesson for COVID
science.summary: Memories of pandemics are often contentious. They can be disputed, uncomfortable and politically charged. As the COVID-19 pandemic begins to feel more distant, governments, communities and families have started asking how it should be remembered.

science.title: Atomic‐scale tracing of lithium trapped in copper current collectors
science.summary: Any loss of lithium reduces the capacity and service life of lithium batteries. Recent research suggested that lithium is lost to the current collector during charging. Researchers at Ruhr University Bochum, Germany, working with Professor Tong Li at the Helmholtz Institute Ulm and a team led by Professor Dominic Bresser at the Karlsruhe Institute of Technology, Germany, took a closer look at this hypothesis.

science.title: Chitosan-based hydrogel membranes as transparent biomaterials for skin regeneration
science.summary: IMDEA Materials Institute has developed mechanically tunable hydrogel membranes that closely mimic the mechanical environment of human skin while remaining highly biocompatible, representing an improved platform for skin tissue engineering and regenerative medicine.

science.title: Oobleck droplets reveal 5 ways cornstarch 'goo' behaves when hitting water
science.summary: Cornstarch can thicken soup or serve as a base for a DIY shampoo, but there's more to the humble pantry staple. Given the right conditions, it seems to defy the laws of physics. Mixing cornstarch with water creates "oobleck"—a shape-shifting substance classified as a non-Newtonian fluid that changes states when subjected to a force.

science.title: A 200-year-old physics experiment could help build future computers
science.summary: Scientists at Nanyang Technological University in Singapore have discovered a surprisingly simple way to create exotic light structures called optical skyrmions using a 200-year-old optical effect known as the Poisson spot. Instead of relying on expensive, highly engineered materials, they simply shine a laser at a tiny circular disc, producing stable swirling patterns in light that researchers believe could one day help power advanced data storage, communications, and computing technologies.

science.title: Netflix's 'Heartstopper' sparked global book-buying boom, study finds
science.summary: A Netflix adaptation helped transform "Heartstopper" from a popular graphic novel into a global mainstream publishing success, according to new research from The University of Manchester and Princeton University.

science.title: Hidden fifth dimension could tune dark matter resonance, new theory proposes
science.summary: The mysterious substance that binds galaxies together could naturally be "in tune" with a hidden fifth dimension, according to a new University of Sheffield theory aiming to shed light on one of science's biggest enigmas: dark matter.

science.title: Firefly brightness holds a cautionary tale about accepting older measurements
science.summary: For over a century, the accepted value for a firefly's brightness has mostly stood, tracing its origins to experiments carried out in 1912. Through rigorous new analysis published in the American Journal of Physics, David Silver of Remiza AI in New York has discovered that this value has likely been vastly overestimated. His results provide a stark reminder of what can happen when widely accepted older measurements are converted into modern standard units.

science.title: How the 'creeping normality' of large language models is quietly reshaping the life sciences
science.summary: Large language models (LLMs) are gradually transforming research in the life sciences in ways that extend far beyond improving productivity, and they are becoming a new normal before scientists have agreed on the limits of their use.

science.title: Research reveals how grassroots football could help tackle climate change
science.summary: With international football facing increasing criticism over its carbon footprint, University of Bath students are working with Bath City FC to show how community football could help tackle climate change.

science.title: Quantum-gravitational mechanism could explain the universe's homogeneity
science.summary: Our universe is known to be remarkably homogeneous and isotropic. This essentially means that matter is distributed evenly throughout the universe and that it looks almost the same in all directions.

science.title: Stephen Hawking's black hole laws just got a major upgrade
science.summary: Scientists have developed a new framework that could finally apply the laws of thermodynamics to real, ever-changing black holes instead of only perfectly stable ones. The advance may improve our understanding of black hole mergers, evaporation, and the powerful gravitational wave events detected by observatories like LIGO.

science.title: Physicists say quantum mechanics may not need imaginary numbers after all
science.summary: Physicists from Heinrich Heine University Düsseldorf (HHU) have examined a fundamental property of quantum mechanics in collaboration with the German Aerospace Center (DLR). In the scientific journal Physical Review Letters, they show that this theory does not necessarily need to be formulated with imaginary numbers – real numbers can in fact also be used. The American Physical Society has also dedicated a “Highlight” to these findings in its Physics Magazine.

environment.title: Bornean ferret badger only lives in Borneo. Could it be a conservation symbol?
environment.summary: The Bornean ferret badger is a small carnivore with the slinky body of a ferret and a face mask like a badger. A new study confirms that it lives only in the mountains of Sabah, a Malaysian state on the island of Borneo. Ferret badgers are nocturnal carnivores, widespread across Southeast Asia, but the Bornean […]

environment.title: Mozambique completes first white rhino breeding population in decades
environment.summary: On June 6, nine female white rhinos arrived in Mozambique’s Zinave National Park following a two-day translocation. Their arrival marks the culmination of nearly 10 years of rhino reintroduction efforts in the park, aimed at rebuilding a viable breeding population of the mammals in Zinave after decades of local extinction. The white rhinos (Ceratotherium simum) […]

environment.title: ‘Flamingo Revolution’ aims to stop Kushner-backed resort on protected Albanian delta
environment.summary: VJOSA-NARTA, Albania — In late April, heavy machinery began moving into the Pishë Poro-Narta protected landscape on Albania’s Adriatic coast without permits or public notice. Bulldozers and excavators felled coastal pine trees, flattened sand dunes, and cut new roads through previously untouched habitat. Then, barbed wire fences went up along the shoreline. The incursion was […]

environment.title: The secret underground system keeping the Grand Canyon alive
environment.summary: Scientists are venturing into the Grand Canyon’s hidden cave networks to solve a mystery: how snowmelt travels underground to supply the park’s vital springs. Their discoveries could help protect the canyon’s water from drought, contamination, and other growing threats.

environment.title: DNA solves 250-year-old mystery of the Seychelles’ lost crocodiles
environment.summary: Scientists have solved the mystery of the Seychelles’ vanished crocodiles using DNA from historic museum specimens. The reptiles were not a unique species after all, but an isolated population of saltwater crocodiles that likely drifted thousands of kilometers across the Indian Ocean.

environment.title: Scottish wrens may be evolving into new species through island gigantism
environment.summary: Tiny birds on remote Scottish islands are undergoing a dramatic evolutionary transformation. Scientists studying four isolated populations of British Wrens discovered that some island birds have grown astonishingly large — with the biggest St Kilda Wrens weighing more than twice as much as the smallest mainland birds. The research suggests these wrens are evolving independently, developing unique songs, appearances, and genetics that may eventually turn them into entirely new species.

environment.title: A New York cemetery was hiding 5.5 million bees underground
environment.summary: A casual walk through an Ithaca cemetery led to the discovery of a gigantic hidden bee population — roughly 5.5 million ground-nesting bees packed beneath the soil. Scientists believe it may be one of the largest bee aggregations ever documented and say the insects are crucial pollinators for apple orchards and other crops. The bees have likely lived there for more than 100 years, thriving in the cemetery’s undisturbed sandy soil.

environment.title: Earth’s orbital wobble triggered rapid climate chaos during the dinosaur age
environment.summary: New research suggests Earth’s climate can swing wildly on surprisingly short timescales — even during hot, ice-free greenhouse periods. By studying ancient sediments from the Late Cretaceous, scientists uncovered repeating climate shifts tied to tiny changes in Earth’s orbital wobble. These cycles may have repeatedly pushed the planet between humid and arid states every few thousand years.

mix_items.title: Sit+Read POP—UP Fair aduce la Timișoara două zile dedicate creației independente și artiștilor locali
mix_items.summary: În perioada 1–2 august 2026, între orele 12:00 și 19:00, Indecis Artist-Run (Bastion 3, str. Hector nr. 1) găzduiește Sit+Read POP—UP Fair, un eveniment care reunește artiști, designeri și creatori… Articolul Sit+Read POP—UP Fair aduce la Timișoara două zile dedicate creației independente și artiștilor locali apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

mix_items.summary: Concert de gală la Ravello Festival, dirijat de Daniel Jinga în încheierea semestrului românesc al Anului Cultural România – Italia 2026. Orchestra Operei Naționale București, dirijată de Daniel Jinga, a… Articolul Orchestra Operei Naționale București, decorată cu Medalia Camerei Deputaților a Republicii Italiene. Concert de gală la Ravello Festival, dirijat de Daniel Jinga în încheierea semestrului românesc al Anului Cultural România – Italia 2026. apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

mix_items.title: Programul organizat de WIPO și Rubik Hub, WIPO IP Management Clinic, a ajuns la final
mix_items.summary: Organizația Mondială a Proprietății Intelectuale (WIPO), împreună cu Rubik Hub, Digital Innovation Zone (DIZ), IMAGO-MOL Cluster și Innovate EU, a lansat în martie 2026 programul WIPO x Rubik Hub IP… Articolul Programul organizat de WIPO și Rubik Hub, WIPO IP Management Clinic, a ajuns la final apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

mix_items.title: 😂 Pe Via Transilvanica au fost montate televizoare imense, să poți vedea meciuri în timp ce mergi pe jos
mix_items.summary: Pentru ca turiștii de pe Via Transilvanica să nu rateze meciurile de la diversele campionate de fotbal din lume, pe marginea traseului de drumeție au fost montate televizoare uriașe, alimentate cu panouri solare și baterii. Din păcate turiștii nu vor putea schimba canalele, pentru că exista riscul ca familiile cu copii să mute pe Tra La […] The post Pe Via Transilvanica au fost montate televizoare imense, să poți vedea meciuri în timp ce mergi pe jos appeared first on Times New Roman .

mix_items.title: Eroul Spaniei la Cupa Davis din 2019 a anunțat că și-a încheiat cariera
mix_items.summary: Tenismanul spaniol Roberto Bautista Agut a disputat în acest weekend ultimul său turneu profesionist, punând astfel capăt unei cariere îndelungate, de peste două decenii, un parcurs în care a devenit eroul victoriei Spaniei în finala Cupei Davis din 2019, printre alte realizări importante, relatează agenția EFE, citată de Agerpres. Istoricul turneu Copa del Rey de …

mix_items.title: Inocenți, locul în care curajul crește pas cu pas
mix_items.summary: Descoperă povestea Alinei și cum sprijinul oferit de Fundația Inocenți a ajutat-o să își dezvolte încrederea în sine, abilitățile școlare și curajul de a reuși. The post Inocenți, locul în care curajul crește pas cu pas appeared first on Fundația Inocenți .

mix_items.title: Vestul nu se lasă și vrea să creeze un nou unicorn românesc. Care sunt cele 9 startup-uri pe drumul ambițios pavat de Vest Ventures
mix_items.summary: Regiunea Vest a spus cu nenumărate ocazii că vrea să dea țării următorul unicorn românesc. Până în 2030. Mișcările din ultimii doi ani și fondurile recent lansate lucrează la misiunea asta la toate...

mix_items.title: Evaluarea Națională 2026: Tranziția către liceu, punctul critic al abandonului școlar. World Vision România propune un plan de intervenție
mix_items.summary: București, 1 iulie. Rezultatele Evaluării Naționale 2026 reconfirmă decalajele profunde dintre copiii din mediul rural și cei din mediul urban... Articolul Evaluarea Națională 2026: Tranziția către liceu, punctul critic al abandonului școlar. World Vision România propune un plan de intervenție apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.title: Părinte Social
mix_items.summary: Părinte Social (Mamă SOS) – București și Cisnădie, Sibiu Transformă un loc de muncă într-o misiune de viață La SOS Satele Copiilor România, credem că fiecare copil are dreptul să... The post Părinte Social appeared first on SOS Satele Copiilor România .

mix_items.title: Random by design: Flickering genes may spend energy to achieve precision
mix_items.summary: Inside the cell nucleus, genes must be turned on and off with precision to regulate biological processes. The first models of gene regulation were developed in the 1960s, yet modern science continues to uncover new layers of control. A new study involving researchers from the Institute of Science and Technology Austria (ISTA), the Institut Pasteur and Princeton University, published in PNAS, suggests that genes obey an optimal switching principle—random at any given moment, yet precise on average.

mix_items.title: Psiholog
mix_items.summary: Locație: Bacău | Nivel carieră: Experiență de minimum 1 an Candidatul Ideal: Ești tu vocea de care au nevoie? Un copil are nevoie de iubire, siguranță și încredere pentru a... The post Psiholog appeared first on SOS Satele Copiilor România .

mix_items.title: Sondaj World Vision România de Ziua Copilului: aproape un sfert dintre copii și adolescenți consultați spun că adulții îi ascultă rar sau aproape niciodată. Anxietatea și presiunea școlii, principalele probleme ale generației lor.
mix_items.summary: București, 1 iunie 2026. Aproape un sfert dintre adolescenții consultați de World Vision România spun că adulții îi ascultă rar... Articolul Sondaj World Vision România de Ziua Copilului: aproape un sfert dintre copii și adolescenți consultați spun că adulții îi ascultă rar sau aproape niciodată. Anxietatea și presiunea școlii, principalele probleme ale generației lor. apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.title: Novel technology could aid targeted lung cancer treatment
mix_items.summary: Scientists have developed a new method for accurately predicting gene changes that cause lung cancer, without the need for slower, more expensive lab techniques. The technology was able to identify specific genetic changes with high accuracy, offering a potentially faster, more efficient and cheaper testing option than traditional methods.

mix_items.title: Epigenetic therapy shows promise against treatment-resistant AML
mix_items.summary: Researchers at The University of Texas MD Anderson Cancer Center have discovered that an investigational epigenetic therapy remained effective in treatment-resistant acute myeloid leukemia (AML) by activating the Hippo pathway, a tumor-suppressing pathway linked to cancer growth and drug resistance.

mix_items.title: Scientists' credibility depends on audience and perceived motives
mix_items.summary: A new study from Virginia Tech found that scientists' credibility often hinges on their perceived motivations. "Our study finds that perceptions of scientists' motivation to serve the public good are the strongest predictor of whether Americans trust them, accept their information as credible, and support science-based policies," said Dara Wald, associate professor in environmental policy and planning.

mix_items.title: Young blood stem cells rejuvenate aging immune systems in old mice
mix_items.summary: By freezing your own healthy blood stem cells in your 20s, thawing them and undergoing a stem cell transplant in your 40s or 50s, it might be possible to rejuvenate your blood-forming and immune systems. Science fiction? At least it works when old mice receive healthy blood stem cells from young mice.

mix_items.title: As super El Niño draws global attention, the Indian Ocean may hold the key to Mediterranean climate extremes
mix_items.summary: As scientists around the world closely monitor the possible development of a powerful "Super El Niño," a new study suggests that another tropical ocean deserves equal attention. Researchers have found that temperature changes in the Indian Ocean can significantly influence winter weather thousands of kilometers away in the Eastern Mediterranean, offering new opportunities to predict damaging dry spells months before they occur.

mix_items.title: Advanced climate models used to estimate temperature-related suicide patterns by 2050
mix_items.summary: A large international team, including researchers from the University of Tokyo, wanted to know whether and how climate change might increase the number of temperature-related suicides around the world. Previous studies have shown that hotter weather is often linked to a higher risk of suicide, but the present study, published in Nature Mental Health, combined data from an unprecedented 751 locations in 26 countries and used advanced climate models to estimate how the number of temperature-related suicides might change by the 2050s under different climate change scenarios.

mix_items.title: Social media likes may have a bigger influence on people with depression
mix_items.summary: One of the first things many people do after posting on social media is check how many likes they have and who has liked their content. This habit can be an instant mood booster when a post is popular.

mix_items.title: Medieval plague survivors left us graffiti, court records and a lesson for COVID
mix_items.summary: Memories of pandemics are often contentious. They can be disputed, uncomfortable and politically charged. As the COVID-19 pandemic begins to feel more distant, governments, communities and families have started asking how it should be remembered.

mix_items.title: How the brain's chemical messengers control consciousness and sleep
mix_items.summary: Scientists at Newcastle University's Neural Circuits Laboratory, in collaboration with researchers at the Blue Brain Project (EPFL, Switzerland) and leading institutions in Spain, have published a study that advances understanding of the brain's chemical messengers—acetylcholine, dopamine and serotonin—known as neuromodulators.

mix_items.title: Knowledge about genes isn't enough: How to inform people about genetic risk of obesity
mix_items.summary: Does informing people about their genetic risk of obesity help them change their habits and lose excess weight? A study published in the journal Obesity Reviews by scientists, including researchers from SWPS University, shows that knowledge about one's genes alone is not enough. Effective behavioral change requires long-term, personalized support.

mix_items.title: Atomic‐scale tracing of lithium trapped in copper current collectors
mix_items.summary: Any loss of lithium reduces the capacity and service life of lithium batteries. Recent research suggested that lithium is lost to the current collector during charging. Researchers at Ruhr University Bochum, Germany, working with Professor Tong Li at the Helmholtz Institute Ulm and a team led by Professor Dominic Bresser at the Karlsruhe Institute of Technology, Germany, took a closer look at this hypothesis.

mix_items.title: Chitosan-based hydrogel membranes as transparent biomaterials for skin regeneration
mix_items.summary: IMDEA Materials Institute has developed mechanically tunable hydrogel membranes that closely mimic the mechanical environment of human skin while remaining highly biocompatible, representing an improved platform for skin tissue engineering and regenerative medicine.

mix_items.title: Oobleck droplets reveal 5 ways cornstarch 'goo' behaves when hitting water
mix_items.summary: Cornstarch can thicken soup or serve as a base for a DIY shampoo, but there's more to the humble pantry staple. Given the right conditions, it seems to defy the laws of physics. Mixing cornstarch with water creates "oobleck"—a shape-shifting substance classified as a non-Newtonian fluid that changes states when subjected to a force.

mix_items.title: Simple digital check-ins improve outcomes for cancer patients, study finds
mix_items.summary: New results from an Alliance Foundation Trials (AFT) study reveal that a simple weekly electronic symptom check-in with their care team significantly improves the quality of life for individuals undergoing treatment for advanced cancer. The study found that the biggest improvements were seen among patient groups that have historically faced the greatest barriers to care, including Black patients and individuals with less formal education.

mix_items.title: Researchers develop a safer, more reliable material for growing small-scale models of the human gut
mix_items.summary: Scientists can use patient-derived tissue samples to grow miniature versions of human organs, allowing them to test new medications and disease treatments for personalized care.

mix_items.title: A 200-year-old physics experiment could help build future computers
mix_items.summary: Scientists at Nanyang Technological University in Singapore have discovered a surprisingly simple way to create exotic light structures called optical skyrmions using a 200-year-old optical effect known as the Poisson spot. Instead of relying on expensive, highly engineered materials, they simply shine a laser at a tiny circular disc, producing stable swirling patterns in light that researchers believe could one day help power advanced data storage, communications, and computing technologies.

mix_items.title: Netflix's 'Heartstopper' sparked global book-buying boom, study finds
mix_items.summary: A Netflix adaptation helped transform "Heartstopper" from a popular graphic novel into a global mainstream publishing success, according to new research from The University of Manchester and Princeton University.

mix_items.title: Watching nature videos can help relieve stress, study finds
mix_items.summary: When stressed, it's important to find healthy ways to cope. Getting outdoors is a proven way to do so. But not everyone has easy access to nature. That's where nature videos might help, according to a North Carolina State University professor.

mix_items.title: Hidden fifth dimension could tune dark matter resonance, new theory proposes
mix_items.summary: The mysterious substance that binds galaxies together could naturally be "in tune" with a hidden fifth dimension, according to a new University of Sheffield theory aiming to shed light on one of science's biggest enigmas: dark matter.

mix_items.title: Enhancing safety of advanced radiotherapy cancer treatments for children
mix_items.summary: Researchers from the National Physical Laboratory (NPL), Heidelberg University Hospital and the German Cancer Research Center (DKFZ) have published new findings that improve understanding of secondary neutron exposure during pediatric ion beam therapy, an important area of study for enhancing the safety of advanced cancer treatments for children.

mix_items.title: Firefly brightness holds a cautionary tale about accepting older measurements
mix_items.summary: For over a century, the accepted value for a firefly's brightness has mostly stood, tracing its origins to experiments carried out in 1912. Through rigorous new analysis published in the American Journal of Physics, David Silver of Remiza AI in New York has discovered that this value has likely been vastly overestimated. His results provide a stark reminder of what can happen when widely accepted older measurements are converted into modern standard units.

mix_items.title: How the 'creeping normality' of large language models is quietly reshaping the life sciences
mix_items.summary: Large language models (LLMs) are gradually transforming research in the life sciences in ways that extend far beyond improving productivity, and they are becoming a new normal before scientists have agreed on the limits of their use.

mix_items.title: Cellular recycling protein plays critical protective role in the gut
mix_items.summary: Australian researchers have discovered that even a modest reduction in the protein BECLIN1 leads to a significant increase in gut inflammation, which can lead to further disease and complications over time.

mix_items.title: Research reveals how grassroots football could help tackle climate change
mix_items.summary: With international football facing increasing criticism over its carbon footprint, University of Bath students are working with Bath City FC to show how community football could help tackle climate change.

mix_items.title: The importance of human review in AI-driven physicians' documentation
mix_items.summary: Artificial intelligence in clinical settings is becoming more common. AI speech-to-text had a moment in the spotlight on the medical drama series "The Pitt" earlier this year. "Studies show that you can spend 80% less time charting," says character Baran Al-Hashimi, a doctor showing her peers a new AI speech-to-text tool. But a skeptical colleague raised concerns after the tool misheard the name of a patient's medication.

mix_items.title: Insurance-supported integrative oncology program improves patients' symptoms
mix_items.summary: A new study from University Hospitals Connor Whole Health reports that an insurance-supported integrative oncology program resulted in clinically meaningful improvements in cancer-related symptoms like pain, stress, anxiety, depression, nausea and fatigue. The findings highlight a scalable model with potential for delivering future integrative therapies within routine oncology care. The findings from this study were recently published in JCO Oncology Practice.

mix_items.title: Quantum-gravitational mechanism could explain the universe's homogeneity
mix_items.summary: Our universe is known to be remarkably homogeneous and isotropic. This essentially means that matter is distributed evenly throughout the universe and that it looks almost the same in all directions.

mix_items.title: Why are healthy young non-smokers developing lung cancer?
mix_items.summary: An unexpected study found that young non-smokers with healthier diets had higher rates of lung cancer, raising questions about whether pesticide exposure from conventionally grown produce could play a role. Researchers stress that the findings are preliminary and require further studies before any conclusions can be drawn.

mix_items.title: Stephen Hawking's black hole laws just got a major upgrade
mix_items.summary: Scientists have developed a new framework that could finally apply the laws of thermodynamics to real, ever-changing black holes instead of only perfectly stable ones. The advance may improve our understanding of black hole mergers, evaporation, and the powerful gravitational wave events detected by observatories like LIGO.

mix_items.title: Where you live could shape your dementia risk, massive study finds
mix_items.summary: Researchers analyzing data from more than 214,000 people found that dementia risk factors differ widely across countries, challenging the idea of a one-size-fits-all prevention strategy. At the same time, they uncovered surprisingly consistent patterns that could help shape smarter, more targeted public health efforts.

mix_items.title: Physicists say quantum mechanics may not need imaginary numbers after all
mix_items.summary: Physicists from Heinrich Heine University Düsseldorf (HHU) have examined a fundamental property of quantum mechanics in collaboration with the German Aerospace Center (DLR). In the scientific journal Physical Review Letters, they show that this theory does not necessarily need to be formulated with imaginary numbers – real numbers can in fact also be used. The American Physical Society has also dedicated a “Highlight” to these findings in its Physics Magazine.

mix_items.title: Bornean ferret badger only lives in Borneo. Could it be a conservation symbol?
mix_items.summary: The Bornean ferret badger is a small carnivore with the slinky body of a ferret and a face mask like a badger. A new study confirms that it lives only in the mountains of Sabah, a Malaysian state on the island of Borneo. Ferret badgers are nocturnal carnivores, widespread across Southeast Asia, but the Bornean […]

mix_items.title: Mozambique completes first white rhino breeding population in decades
mix_items.summary: On June 6, nine female white rhinos arrived in Mozambique’s Zinave National Park following a two-day translocation. Their arrival marks the culmination of nearly 10 years of rhino reintroduction efforts in the park, aimed at rebuilding a viable breeding population of the mammals in Zinave after decades of local extinction. The white rhinos (Ceratotherium simum) […]

mix_items.title: ‘Flamingo Revolution’ aims to stop Kushner-backed resort on protected Albanian delta
mix_items.summary: VJOSA-NARTA, Albania — In late April, heavy machinery began moving into the Pishë Poro-Narta protected landscape on Albania’s Adriatic coast without permits or public notice. Bulldozers and excavators felled coastal pine trees, flattened sand dunes, and cut new roads through previously untouched habitat. Then, barbed wire fences went up along the shoreline. The incursion was […]

mix_items.title: The secret underground system keeping the Grand Canyon alive
mix_items.summary: Scientists are venturing into the Grand Canyon’s hidden cave networks to solve a mystery: how snowmelt travels underground to supply the park’s vital springs. Their discoveries could help protect the canyon’s water from drought, contamination, and other growing threats.

mix_items.title: DNA solves 250-year-old mystery of the Seychelles’ lost crocodiles
mix_items.summary: Scientists have solved the mystery of the Seychelles’ vanished crocodiles using DNA from historic museum specimens. The reptiles were not a unique species after all, but an isolated population of saltwater crocodiles that likely drifted thousands of kilometers across the Indian Ocean.

mix_items.title: Scottish wrens may be evolving into new species through island gigantism
mix_items.summary: Tiny birds on remote Scottish islands are undergoing a dramatic evolutionary transformation. Scientists studying four isolated populations of British Wrens discovered that some island birds have grown astonishingly large — with the biggest St Kilda Wrens weighing more than twice as much as the smallest mainland birds. The research suggests these wrens are evolving independently, developing unique songs, appearances, and genetics that may eventually turn them into entirely new species.

mix_items.title: A New York cemetery was hiding 5.5 million bees underground
mix_items.summary: A casual walk through an Ithaca cemetery led to the discovery of a gigantic hidden bee population — roughly 5.5 million ground-nesting bees packed beneath the soil. Scientists believe it may be one of the largest bee aggregations ever documented and say the insects are crucial pollinators for apple orchards and other crops. The bees have likely lived there for more than 100 years, thriving in the cemetery’s undisturbed sandy soil.

mix_items.title: Earth’s orbital wobble triggered rapid climate chaos during the dinosaur age
mix_items.summary: New research suggests Earth’s climate can swing wildly on surprisingly short timescales — even during hot, ice-free greenhouse periods. By studying ancient sediments from the Late Cretaceous, scientists uncovered repeating climate shifts tied to tiny changes in Earth’s orbital wobble. These cycles may have repeatedly pushed the planet between humid and arid states every few thousand years.

joke_ro.text: Azi sunt foarte zen. N-am energie nici sa ma stresez.

satire_ro.title: Piaţa maşinilor second, în scădere. VW concediază 50.000 de bătrânici care conduceau doar duminica
